title: Direction of Steepest Ascent
concept: The gradient is a vector that groups all partial derivatives together, pointing in the direction of the steepest slope uphill.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a partial derivative?

options.text: The slope with respect to one input while holding other inputs constant
A derivative that is only half correct
The average rate of change for all inputs combined

exercises.explain: Spot on! A partial derivative isolates one knob. But what happens when we combine all these individual slopes?

cards.title: Hiking in the fog
cards.body: Imagine you are hiking up a mountain in dense fog. You can't see the peak, but you can feel the slope of the ground under your feet. Which way is "up"?

cards.title: What is the gradient?
cards.body: In calculus, the gradient is a vector (a list of numbers) containing all the partial derivatives. It points in the direction of the steepest ascent — straight uphill.

cards.title: The uphill compass
cards.body: Think of the gradient like a compass that always points directly uphill. If you want to climb as fast as possible, you just walk in the exact direction the compass needle is pointing.

cards.title: A 2D gradient vector
cards.body: If your model's error gradient is [3, -4], it means the steepest path to increase error is by moving +3 units in the first weight and -4 in the second. To decrease error, you go the opposite way.

cards.title: Not a peak detector
cards.body: The gradient doesn't tell you where the peak actually is. It only tells you the local direction of the steepest climb from your current spot.

cards.title: Uphill vs. downhill
cards.body: Comparing the gradient and its opposite:

exercises.prompt: What does the gradient vector contain?

options.text: The partial derivatives for all inputs
Only the average of the inputs

exercises.explain: The gradient is a package of all the partial derivatives, representing the slope in every direction.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The gradient points in the direction of the steepest descent (straight downhill).
exercises.explain: False! The gradient points straight uphill (steepest ascent). To go downhill, we walk in the opposite direction.

exercises.sentence: The gradient vector always points in the direction of steepest ___.
exercises.explain: The gradient points in the direction of the steepest climb (ascent) uphill.

exercises.prompt: Which arrow shows the direction of the gradient on a hill?

options.text: An arrow pointing straight up the steepest part of the slope
An arrow pointing straight down to the bottom of the valley
An arrow pointing sideways along a flat path

exercises.explain: The gradient always points in the direction that climbs the slope most rapidly.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a path for a robot to climb a rugged mountain. At its current position, the gradient vector is [2, 5]. What direction should the robot move to climb the mountain as quickly as possible?

options.text: Move +2 in the X direction and +5 in the Y direction
Move -2 in the X direction and -5 in the Y direction
Move +5 in the X direction and +2 in the Y direction

exercises.explain: Spectacular! Moving in the exact direction of the gradient vector [2, 5] ensures the steepest possible climb. You've mastered the concept of the gradient and steepest ascent! Next up: combining these to optimize models!
